title: Clifford-deformed zero-rate LDPC codes with 50% biased noise thresholds
authors: Jagannath Das, Sayandip Dhara, Pedro Medina, Arthur Pesah, Arpit Dua
abstract: Applying single-qubit Clifford unitaries to a Pauli stabilizer code produces a Clifford-deformed variant whose stabilizers remain Pauli operators, but with locally rotated Pauli axes. Such deformations provide a simple way to tailor a fixed code to anisotropic noise, and have enabled unusually high thresholds under strongly biased dephasing. In this work, we discuss zero-rate quantum low-density parity-check (LDPC) codes, for which there exist Clifford-deformed variants where the number of biased logical operators scales slower than the distance, or there exists a basis of logical operators whose overlap satisfies certain scaling conditions; in this case, the code-capacity threshold for the Clifford-deformed variant under i.i.d. pure dephasing noise approaches 50%. This property provably explains previously known code examples with 50% biased noise thresholds, such as XY surface code, XZZX surface code, color code, as well as some 3D Clifford-deformed codes. As a concrete new example, we study Clifford deformations of the tile codes of Ref. [1]. Similar to the phase diagram of 50% thresholds for random Clifford deformations of the surface code in Ref. [2], we find a similar phase diagram for the tile codes. We also construct several translationally invariant deformations of the tile code with 50% thresholds, and present numerical evidence for improved performance at finite bias and under circuit-level noise. In the circuit-level setting, performance is governed by the residual bias after a full syndrome-extraction cycle, linking our simulations to phenomenological models commonly used to study Clifford-deformed codes. We estimate this residual bias for different qubit platforms by modeling microscopic implementations of tile-code syndrome extraction.
summary: This work establishes the theoretical conditions under which Clifford-deformed zero-rate LDPC codes can achieve a 50% code-capacity threshold when subjected to pure dephasing noise. By analyzing the scaling of logical operators, the authors unify the understanding of several known high-threshold codes. The analysis extends to finite-bias and circuit-level noise, providing practical guidelines for hardware implementation.
main_problem: The paper aims to derive general conditions for zero-rate quantum LDPC codes to achieve a 50% code-capacity threshold under i.i.d. pure dephasing noise.
main_result: The 50% threshold is provably achievable for LDPC codes if the number of pure-Z logical operators grows subexponentially or if a non-overlapping basis of logical operators exists.
method: The authors use analytical bounds, including a union-bound criterion and a sharper bound based on the Basis of Logical Operators (BLO), alongside numerical simulations for finite-bias and circuit-level noise.
model_or_system: The study focuses on Clifford-deformed variants of zero-rate LDPC codes, which are subjected to anisotropic noise dominated by pure dephasing (Z errors).
key_observables: Code-capacity threshold (approaching 50%), logical failure probability decay, and residual bias in circuit-level noise.
important_parameters: Pure dephasing noise probability ($p_Z$), code rate (zero-rate limit), and the scaling of logical operators vs. distance.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the existence of specific structures within the logical operator space (e.g., non-overlapping BLO) and often assumes the infinite-bias limit for analytical proofs.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate phase diagrams showing regions of 50% thresholds for random deformations, and performance plots comparing thresholds for different structured deformations (Linear, TI, XY).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the general theoretical conditions for the 50% threshold using bounds, to applying these conditions to concrete examples like tile codes, and finally extending the analysis to finite-bias and circuit-level noise simulations.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful, general theoretical framework linking code structure (LDPC, Clifford deformation) directly to a specific, physically relevant noise threshold (50% for dephasing), which is crucial for designing fault-tolerant quantum memories.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Stabilizers of the [[288, 8, 12]] tile code and its Clifford-deformed variants. In the undeformed tile code, qubits reside on the edges of the lattice, and dots denote stabilizer generators. Boundary dots correspond to single-type stabilizers: mint-green dots denote Z-type stabilizers and orange dots denote X-type stabilizers. Bulk black dots indicate locations where both X- and Z-type stabilizers are present. The support of each stabilizer on nearby qubits is illustrated pictorially. In the bulk, stabilizer generators have weight six, acting on three vertical and three horizontal qubits, as in (a). At the boundaries, truncated geometry produces stabilizers of reduced weight (2, 3,...

FIG. 2: Phase diagram of periodic tile codes is shown under random Clifford deformation at infinite bias (η → ∞). The axes (ΠXZ, ΠY Z) specify the probabilities of ap- plying local Clifford permutations that swap X ↔Z and Y ↔Z, respectively. Colors indicate regions with differ- ent estimated thresholds. The rhombic region bounded by the phase points (0.5, 0), (0.5, 0.5), (0, 1), and (0, 0.5) exhibits a 50% threshold. Along the white boundary, we do not observe a sharp crossing in the threshold plot for most of the phase points. Moving away from this regime, the threshold decreases monotonically. Iso-threshold con- tours appear on the two sides of the rhombic regime, forming vertical lines...

FIG. 3: Translation-invariant realization associated with the phase point (0.25, 0.5) for the open tile code [[72, 8, 4]]. The dotted rectangle denotes a unit cell. The color of any edge reflects the deformation applied to the qubit on that edge. On dark purple (yellow) qubits, we apply Y Z (XZ) deformations, while gray qubits remain undeformed.
FIG. 4: Threshold (pth) as a function of the slope s in the linear scaling |BZ(n)| = sn + c for the phase points shown in Fig. 15 in Appendix. The threshold decreases with increasing s.

Fig. 6 shows an example syndrome-extraction circuit for the linear-deformed tile code (bulk checks shown; boundary checks are analogous but have lower weight). All data qubits are initialized in the |+⟩basis. Check qubits are initialized in the |+⟩basis at the start of each measurement round and are reset to |+⟩after measure- ment. Let V denote the set of vertical data qubits and H the set of horizontal data qubits. In the linear deformation, the vertical data qubits in V are acted on by Hadamard gates H before entangling with check qubits.
